data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The simultaneous collapse resulted from a synchronized climatic shock (the 3.2ka event) that specifically targeted the rain-fed agricultural hinterlands of the Hittite and Mycenaean states, while Egypt's survival is explained by its exclusive reliance on Nile flood irrigation which remained stable.", "mechanism": "Ecological niche specificity: A multi-decadal drought reduced yields in dry-farming zones (Anatolia/Greece) below the threshold required to sustain the non-producing palace bureaucracies. This forced mass migration (Sea Peoples) from the stricken hinterlands toward the coasts and Egypt. The mechanism is the differential vulnerability of dry-farming vs. river-irrigation systems to the same climatic oscillation.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Mycenaean Greece", "Levantine city-states", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Nile Delta settlements"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data (pollen cores, speleothems) indicates normal or above-average precipitation in Central Anatolia and the Peloponnese between 1200-1150 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This sharpens the environmental hypothesis by contrasting specific agricultural technologies (dry vs. irrigated) rather than just stating 'drought', explaining the asymmetry of survival."}
